hq: No physical office — fully remote / cloud-based

system_description.overview: Afterword is a comprehensive, cloud-based SaaS platform for the death care profession, founded in 2019. It provides funeral homes, crematoriums, and cemeteries with a unified suite of tools to manage all aspects of their operations from first call to aftercare.

products.Case and Operations Management: Centralized system for managing funeral home cases including vital statistics, documents, payments, communications, digital whiteboards, task management, automated document/contract generation with e-signature, integrated payment processing, and reporting dashboards with role-based permissions.
products.Family Experience and Collaboration: Online Planner that guides families through arrangement decisions with educational content and merchandise selections. Secure family collaboration tools for remote document sharing and coordination with funeral home staff.
products.Cremation and Transfer Management: Dedicated module for end-to-end chain of custody tracking for decedents with QR/barcode scanning from mobile devices, immutable audit trails with timestamps, user identity, GPS coordinates, and photo capture of decedent personal belongings.
products.Grace AI Assistant: Integrated AI assistant that automates administrative tasks including first call intake, transcribing notes, pre-filling case data, generating obituaries, and managing family follow-up communications.

infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices or servers; fully cloud-based
infrastructure.cdn: Google Cloud Storage (CDN layer for files/assets per architecture diagram)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.waf: GCP firewall rules configured on application gateway and production network
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via GCP in conjunction with security policy network settings
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access through bastion host with VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS encryption for all user requests using certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server for all internet-connected systems
network_architecture.topology: Web Clients and Mobile Apps connect via enforced HTTPS to Auto-scaling Load Balancing, which routes to Firebase/App Engine Web Hosting and Firebase Auth User Service. Backend consists of Cloud Run Functions and Cloud Run/Queues for Bulk Jobs. CDN layer serves Files/Assets from Cloud Storage. Database is Firebase. Backups/DWH stored in Cloud Storage/BigQuery. Integration layer supports REST API and pre-built connectors (QuickBooks, Convey, Google Calendar, ASD, WordPress).

languages: Not explicitly named — implied JavaScript/TypeScript given Firebase and Cloud Run ecosystem
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (CC8.1.4 confirms changes developed and tested in separate environment before implementation)

authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1). Two-factor authentication required for employee email access.
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through GCP Password policy including periodic forced changes, password expiry, and complexity requirements. Accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (GCP KMS implied via CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: GCP cloud provider key management service — access to encryption keys restricted to authorized personnel

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool with MFA access (specific tool not named — likely GitHub given ecosystem)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not explicitly named — formal change management lifecycle documented
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to production environment requires additional approval (CC8.1.2). Approval process followed prior to merging to master branch.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Infrastructure as code — baseline configurations retained within the infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly reviews by CTO. Workstations scanned daily for patch compliance. OS patches managed and applied as they become available.

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not explicitly named — continuous monitoring tools referenced for infrastructure performance
monitoring_logging.siem: Not explicitly named — automated alerts for high and critical risk events; logging at application and infrastructure level
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: GCP logging — logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events. Incident management process invoked for confirmed events.

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure and data center services (subservice organization)
Authentication, database (Firestore), web hosting, App Engine
Answering service integration for funeral homes

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers with continuous automatic scanning. Definitions updated weekly for production servers. Also installed on Mac workstations and laptops.
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications, with frequency adjusted as required. Performed by internal IT Infrastructure team.
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party. Results reviewed by IT management. High/critical findings remediated and added to risk register.

untestable: CC2.2.3 — Significant changes to people, roles and responsibilities could not be tested because there were no significant changes during the engagement period
CC3.2.1 — Cybersecurity insurance could not be tested because there were no cyber incidents reported during the engagement period
CC6.5.2 — Customer data deletion upon termination could not be tested because there were no customer terminations during the engagement period
CC7.3.1 — Incident response policies could not be tested because there were no security incidents reported during the engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to facilities: GCP is responsible for physical security of data centers; not in scope
A1.2 — Environmental protections: Afterword operates from co-working space managed by building management; environmental controls managed by building management / GCP
Processing Integrity — Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy — Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: RGEC (Risk and Governance Executive Committee) meets semiannually to review key internal control measures
IT Leadership Committee meets weekly to review security activities and processes
Board of Directors has independent members and reviews security program status regularly
Formal vendor management with quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author with MFA-protected access to source code tool
Configuration management tool with rollback capability for all infrastructure changes
Immutable audit trails with timestamps, user identity, and GPS coordinates for cremation chain of custody

infrastructure.rationale: GCP with multi-AZ and auto-scaling load balancing is solid. However, single-region (us-central1) with no multi-region failover is a limitation. Serverless compute (Cloud Run, Cloud Functions) is modern. No CDN beyond Cloud Storage mentioned. IaC referenced but specific tooling not named.

application_architecture.rationale: Serverless-heavy architecture on Firebase/Cloud Run is modern and scalable. REST API with pre-built integrations shows maturity. Branch protection and code review enforced. AI assistant (Grace) shows innovation. However, specific languages/frameworks not disclosed, and no mention of automated testing.

data_layer.rationale: Firebase (Firestore) as primary database with BigQuery as data warehouse is a solid GCP-native stack. Daily backups with multi-AZ. Data classification policy in place. Per-tenant segmentation confirmed. No caching layer mentioned.

security_posture.rationale: Strong security posture: WAF, IDS/IPS, annual pen testing, monthly vuln scans, MFA, endpoint protection, antivirus, server hardening, cybersecurity insurance. VPN via bastion host. Content filtering through proxy. Log protection enforced. No SIEM tool explicitly named, and specific security tools not identified by vendor.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC with configuration management and rollback capability. Branch protection with merge approval required. Separate environments confirmed. Emergency change process documented. However, CI/CD tool not named, container orchestration is serverless Cloud Run (simpler), and specific automation tooling is undisclosed.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing with different loss scenarios. Daily backups with multi-AZ failover. However, no multi-region failover, RTO/RPO not disclosed, and DR plan specifics are thin — only references spinning up across AZs within GCP.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Heavy concentration on Google ecosystem (GCP, Firebase, BigQuery, Cloud Run, Cloud Storage, App Engine). Virtually all critical infrastructure depends on a single vendor. Integration partners (QuickBooks, Convey, ASD, WordPress) add some diversity but are not infrastructure-critical.

overall.rationale: Afterword demonstrates a well-governed, security-conscious early-stage company with clean audit results (zero exceptions). The serverless GCP-native architecture is modern and cost-efficient. Key strengths include strong governance (RGEC, ITLC), comprehensive access controls, and innovative product features (AI assistant, immutable chain of custody). Key gaps are single-region deployment, extreme Google vendor concentration, undisclosed RTO/RPO, and lack of named specific tooling for CI/CD, monitoring, and SIEM.

red_flags: Single-region deployment (us-central1 only) — a GCP regional outage would take down the entire platform
Extreme vendor concentration on Google ecosystem — Firebase, Cloud Run, BigQuery, Cloud Storage, App Engine all depend on GCP
RTO/RPO not disclosed despite having a BCDR policy
Very short audit period (3 months: May 6 - August 6, 2025) — this is the minimum for Type 2 and limits confidence in sustained control effectiveness
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria excluded — notable for a platform handling sensitive death care data including personal belongings photos, GPS coordinates, and family information
yellow_flags: Specific CI/CD tooling not named — may indicate immature or manual deployment pipeline
No SIEM tool explicitly identified — monitoring described generically
No caching layer mentioned — may indicate performance concerns at scale
Source code management tool not named — only described generically as 'source code management tool'
No mention of automated testing in the CI/CD pipeline
Cybersecurity insurance control could not be tested (no incidents) — existence not independently verified via policy review
No explicit mention of WAF product — only GCP firewall rules described
Languages and frameworks not disclosed — limits technical assessment
green_flags: Zero exceptions across all 57 controls tested — clean audit
Strong governance structure: Board with independent members, RGEC meeting semiannually, ITLC meeting weekly
MFA enforced for production access and email
Quarterly user access reviews with prompt revocation (within 1 business day)
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and alerts for backup failures
Annual external penetration testing with remediation tracking
Monthly vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications
Formal vendor management program with SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments
Infrastructure as code with rollback capability
Branch protection with merge approval required for production deployments
Data classification policy established and implemented
Innovative product features: AI assistant, immutable chain of custody with GPS tracking
Founded by experienced technology leaders from Google, Shopify, and Workday
key_observations: This is Afterword's first SOC 2 report (3-month observation period suggests initial engagement) — positive that a startup in the death care vertical is pursuing compliance early
The architecture diagram reveals a Firebase-centric stack that is serverless-heavy — cost-efficient for a startup but creates deep platform lock-in to Google
Grace AI Assistant is a differentiating feature that automates first call intake, obituary generation, and case data pre-filling — indicates ML/AI engineering capability
The cremation chain of custody module with QR scanning, GPS coordinates, and immutable audit trails is a sophisticated compliance feature for a niche vertical
Company has no physical office — fully remote with all data in cloud, which simplifies their security boundary but makes cloud provider dependency more acute
Integration layer with REST API and pre-built connectors for QuickBooks, Convey, ASD, and WordPress suggests ecosystem play targeting funeral home back-office workflows

Org chart shows lean structure: CEO, CRO (Business Team), CTO (Technical Team) with Security & SREs, Software & ML Engineers, Designers & UX Research — indicates engineering-first culture for a startup
The report mentions 'Windows/Network team' for patch management which seems inconsistent with a fully cloud/serverless architecture — may be boilerplate from the audit firm template
